Leo: Welcome back to Frame & Light. Today we're talking about roundtable photography, and I'm joined by Ava Lemoine, underwater photographer. Ava Lemoine, thanks for being here.
Ava Lemoine: Thanks, Leo. Great to be back.
Leo: Today's episode is sponsored by Adobe. Adobe Creative Cloud puts Lightroom, Photoshop, and Premiere on every device. Ava Lemoine: Yeah, exactly. And it ties into what we're covering today.
Leo: I want to flag something — Ava mentioned this same dynamic a couple episodes back.I want to flag something — Tariq Hasaan mentioned this same dynamic a couple episodes back.I want to flag something — Daniel Olufemy mentioned this same dynamic a couple episodes back.Real talk: Adobe is the tool I'd pay double for. Not an ad — they're not paying us. I just think it's the best in class for post-processing.I want to dig into that. What's the version of the question that actually matters?
Ava Lemoine: Sure. Roundtables reveal where photographers actually disagree. We've seen this play out at teams using Peak Design. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.
Leo: Take me through the decision. What's the structural angle most listeners don't have access to?
Ava Lemoine: Honestly, Frame and light decisions converge across genres. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.
Leo: Take me through the decision. What's the structural angle most listeners don't have access to?
Ava Lemoine: Here's how I think about it. Practical reliability of gear shows up under crowd-shoot conditions. We've seen this play out at teams using Profoto. And the second-order effect is the part most people miss.
Leo: That tracks.
Ava Lemoine: Outside of Profoto-class tooling, the failure mode is harder to spot. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.
Leo: We'll be right back after a quick word from our sponsors.
Ad: Today's episode is sponsored by Peak Design. Peak Design builds camera bags, straps, and tripods designed by photographers. Visit peakdesign.com/podcast for a free trial.
Leo: Welcome back to the show.What does the failure mode look like? What's something you used to believe about this that you've revised?
Ava Lemoine: Let me give you the version I actually believe. Roundtables reveal where photographers actually disagree. We've seen this play out at teams using Peak Design. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.
Leo: Take me through the decision. What's the most underrated piece of roundtable photography?
Ava Lemoine: Yeah, so — Frame and light decisions converge across genres. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.
Leo: What does the failure mode look like? What's the version of the question that actually matters?
Ava Lemoine: It comes down to this: Practical reliability of gear shows up under crowd-shoot conditions. We've seen this play out at teams using Profoto. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams.
Leo: That tracks.
Ava Lemoine: Peak Design is one of the platforms where this shows up cleanly. And the second-order effect is the part most people miss.
Leo: Let's pivot — strobe lighting is where I want to spend the last stretch.
Ava Lemoine: That's where the conversation gets interesting, because the trade-offs around strobe lighting cross-cut everything we've covered.
Leo: Push back on the conventional wisdom for me. What's the version of the question that actually matters?
Ava Lemoine: It comes down to this: Roundtables reveal where photographers actually disagree. We've seen this play out at teams using Profoto. And the second-order effect is the part most people miss.
Leo: I want to dig into that. Where do frame and roundtable photography intersect?
Ava Lemoine: It comes down to this: Frame and light decisions converge across genres. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're making.
Leo: Walk me through a concrete example. How does Peak Design fit into this picture?
Ava Lemoine: Here's how I think about it. Practical reliability of gear shows up under crowd-shoot conditions. We've seen this play out at teams using Profoto. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.
Leo: That tracks.
Ava Lemoine: Outside of Peak Design-class tooling, the failure mode is harder to spot. And the second-order effect is the part most people miss.
Leo: Before we wrap, what's one thing listeners can try this week?
Ava Lemoine: Pick one of the ideas we covered and run it for two weeks. The rest is iteration.
